title: A Job Offer Discussion
summary: Claire has received a job offer from another company and meets with her manager, Robert, to discuss it. They talk about her concerns, what the company can offer, and how she might make her decision.

body.speaker: Claire
body.text: Thanks for making time for this, Robert. I know it was short notice. I wanted to talk to you because I've received an offer from another company, and I haven't decided what to do yet.

body.speaker: Robert
body.text: I appreciate you coming to me directly, Claire. That takes honesty. Can you tell me a bit about the offer — what kind of role is it?

body.speaker: Claire
body.text: It's a senior analyst position at a mid-sized consultancy. The salary is about fifteen percent higher than what I'm on now, and there's a clear path to a management role within two years.

body.speaker: Robert
body.text: That's a meaningful offer. I won't pretend otherwise. Can I ask — what's prompting you to consider it? Is it mainly the salary, or are there other things that have been on your mind?

body.speaker: Claire
body.text: Honestly, it's a combination of things. The salary matters, but it's not the main driver. I feel like I've been doing similar work for about eighteen months without a significant change in scope or responsibility. I want to feel like I'm growing.

body.speaker: Robert
body.text: That's fair feedback, and I hear you. I want to be direct — some of what you're describing is something we can address here. There's a restructuring planned for Q3 that I haven't been able to discuss externally yet, and your name has genuinely come up in those conversations.

body.speaker: Claire
body.text: I didn't know that. Can you say any more, or is it too early?

body.speaker: Robert
body.text: I can say that there will be a new team lead role created within the analytics function, and you'd be a strong candidate. I can't make a firm commitment today, but I can push for an accelerated conversation internally this week.

body.speaker: Claire
body.text: I'd really appreciate that. The honest truth is I'd prefer to stay here if the trajectory looks right. I've built relationships with this team that I genuinely value, and I know the work well.

body.speaker: Robert
body.text: That means a lot, and I don't want to lose you. Let me also ask — is there anything beyond career progression that's been weighing on you? Workload, flexibility, anything like that?

body.speaker: Claire
body.text: The workload is manageable. One thing I'd mention is flexibility around working from home. Since the return-to-office policy changed, I've found the commute adds a lot of stress to my week. The other company offers two extra remote days per week.

body.speaker: Robert
body.text: That's something I can look into. The policy is set at the company level, but there are exceptions being approved for certain roles and circumstances. I'd be willing to make a case for you.

body.speaker: Claire
body.text: I'd be genuinely grateful for that. How long do you think you'd need to come back to me with something concrete?

body.speaker: Robert
body.text: Give me until Thursday. I'll speak to HR and to the director by then. I just need to know — do you have a deadline on the other offer?

body.speaker: Claire
body.text: They've asked for a response by next Monday, so Thursday works perfectly. I want to make the right choice, not just the fast one.

body.speaker: Robert
body.text: That's exactly the right way to approach it. I'll be in touch by Thursday afternoon, Claire. Thank you for being open with me.

annotations.meaningVi: thông báo gấp, thông báo không trước nhiều
annotations.exampleEn: Thank you for coming at such short notice.

annotations.meaningVi: lý do chính, động lực chủ yếu
annotations.exampleEn: Cost was not the main driver behind the decision.

annotations.meaningVi: thay đổi về phạm vi công việc
annotations.exampleEn: After the promotion, there was a clear change in scope and responsibility.

annotations.meaningVi: giải quyết, xử lý vấn đề
annotations.exampleEn: I'd like to address some of the concerns raised in the meeting.
The company announced a major restructuring that affected three departments.

annotations.meaningVi: cam kết chắc chắn, cam kết rõ ràng
annotations.exampleEn: He couldn't make a firm commitment until the board approved the budget.

annotations.meaningVi: cuộc thảo luận được đẩy nhanh tiến độ
annotations.exampleEn: We had an accelerated conversation to meet the project deadline.

annotations.meaningVi: quỹ đạo phát triển, hướng đi sự nghiệp
annotations.exampleEn: She was happy with her career trajectory after the last promotion.

annotations.meaningVi: đang làm phiền muộn, đang nặng lòng
annotations.exampleEn: The unfinished work has been weighing on me all weekend.

annotations.meaningVi: chính sách quay trở lại làm việc tại văn phòng
annotations.exampleEn: The new return-to-office policy requires staff to come in four days a week.

annotations.meaningVi: các trường hợp ngoại lệ được chấp thuận
annotations.exampleEn: The manager confirmed that exceptions were being approved for medical reasons.

annotations.meaningVi: đưa ra lý lẽ bảo vệ, vận động ủng hộ
annotations.exampleEn: She made a case for extending the project deadline.
Take some time to think about it and come back to me by Friday.

annotations.meaningVi: cụ thể, rõ ràng, không mơ hồ
annotations.exampleEn: We need concrete plans, not just vague ideas.

annotations.meaningVi: hạn chót để trả lời đề nghị
annotations.exampleEn: They put a deadline on the offer, so she had to decide quickly.

annotations.meaningVi: thành thật với, cởi mở chia sẻ với
annotations.exampleEn: Thank you for being open with me about how you're feeling.

grammarNotes.title: Indirect Speech and Hedging — Professional Conversations
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong các cuộc trò chuyện nghề nghiệp nhạy cảm, người nói thường dùng ngôn ngữ gián tiếp để làm mềm tuyên bố và thể hiện sự tôn trọng. Robert nói 'I won't pretend otherwise' thay vì nói thẳng 'It's a great offer', và 'I can't make a firm commitment today' thay vì nói 'No, not yet'. Claire nói 'I'd really appreciate that' thay vì 'Please do that.' Học viên B1 nên chú ý đến cách dùng 'would', 'could', và cụm từ như 'I'd mention' hay 'I'd be willing' để tạo giọng điệu lịch sự, chuyên nghiệp.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In sensitive professional conversations, speakers use indirect language to soften statements and show respect. Robert says 'I won't pretend otherwise' rather than 'It's a great offer', and 'I can't make a firm commitment today' rather than a flat refusal. Claire says 'I'd really appreciate that' rather than a direct command. B1 learners should notice how 'would', 'could', and phrases like 'I'd mention' or 'I'd be willing' signal a polite, professional register.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect — Recent and Ongoing Relevance
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cả hai người dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để liên kết quá khứ với hiện tại: 'I've received an offer', 'I've built relationships', 'I've found the commute adds a lot of stress'. Điểm mấu chốt: thì này được dùng khi sự kiện quá khứ vẫn còn ảnh hưởng đến quyết định hoặc cảm xúc hiện tại. Đây khác với quá khứ đơn, vốn chỉ đơn giản nêu sự kiện đã kết thúc: 'She got an offer yesterday' (đơn thuần là thông tin) so với 'She's received an offer' (còn liên quan đến hiện tại).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Both speakers use the present perfect to connect past events to their present relevance: 'I've received an offer', 'I've built relationships', 'I've found the commute adds a lot of stress'. Key point: this tense is used when a past event still affects current decisions or feelings. Contrast with the past simple, which reports a completed event without linking it to now: 'She got an offer yesterday' (neutral fact) vs 'She's received an offer' (still relevant now).

translationVi:
Claire: Cảm ơn anh đã dành thời gian cho điều này, Robert. Tôi biết đây là thông báo gấp. Tôi muốn nói chuyện với anh vì tôi đã nhận được một đề nghị từ công ty khác, và tôi chưa quyết định phải làm gì.
Robert: Tôi đánh giá cao việc chị đến gặp tôi trực tiếp, Claire. Điều đó thể hiện sự thành thật. Chị có thể cho tôi biết đôi điều về đề nghị đó không — đó là vị trí gì?
Claire: Đó là vị trí chuyên viên phân tích cấp cao tại một công ty tư vấn quy mô vừa. Mức lương cao hơn khoảng mười lăm phần trăm so với mức hiện tại của tôi, và có lộ trình rõ ràng lên vị trí quản lý trong vòng hai năm.
Robert: Đó là một đề nghị có giá trị. Tôi sẽ không giả vờ khác đi. Tôi có thể hỏi — điều gì đang khiến chị cân nhắc điều này? Chủ yếu là do mức lương, hay còn có những điều khác đang ở trong tâm trí chị?
Claire: Thành thật mà nói, đó là sự kết hợp của nhiều thứ. Mức lương quan trọng, nhưng không phải lý do chính. Tôi cảm thấy như mình đã làm công việc tương tự trong khoảng mười tám tháng mà không có sự thay đổi đáng kể về phạm vi hay trách nhiệm. Tôi muốn cảm thấy mình đang phát triển.
Robert: Đó là phản hồi công bằng, và tôi nghe thấy điều đó. Tôi muốn nói thẳng — một số điều chị mô tả là thứ chúng ta có thể giải quyết ở đây. Có một kế hoạch tái cơ cấu vào Quý 3 mà tôi chưa thể thảo luận bên ngoài, và tên chị thực sự đã được nhắc đến trong những cuộc trò chuyện đó.
Claire: Tôi không biết điều đó. Anh có thể nói thêm không, hay còn quá sớm?
Robert: Tôi có thể nói rằng sẽ có một vai trò trưởng nhóm mới được tạo ra trong bộ phận phân tích, và chị sẽ là ứng viên sáng giá. Tôi không thể cam kết chắc chắn hôm nay, nhưng tôi có thể thúc đẩy một cuộc thảo luận nội bộ nhanh hơn trong tuần này.
Claire: Tôi thực sự đánh giá cao điều đó. Thực ra tôi muốn ở lại đây nếu hướng đi phát triển có vẻ đúng. Tôi đã xây dựng được mối quan hệ với nhóm này mà tôi thực sự trân trọng, và tôi hiểu rõ công việc.
Robert: Điều đó có ý nghĩa rất lớn, và tôi không muốn mất chị. Hãy để tôi hỏi thêm — có điều gì ngoài sự thăng tiến nghề nghiệp đang làm phiền chị không? Khối lượng công việc, sự linh hoạt, hay điều gì đó tương tự?
Claire: Khối lượng công việc có thể quản lý được. Một điều tôi muốn đề cập là sự linh hoạt trong việc làm việc tại nhà. Kể từ khi chính sách quay lại văn phòng thay đổi, tôi thấy việc đi lại thêm rất nhiều căng thẳng vào tuần làm việc của mình. Công ty kia cung cấp thêm hai ngày làm việc từ xa mỗi tuần.
Robert: Đó là điều tôi có thể tìm hiểu. Chính sách được đặt ở cấp công ty, nhưng có những trường hợp ngoại lệ đang được chấp thuận cho các vai trò và hoàn cảnh nhất định. Tôi sẵn sàng đưa ra lý lẽ bảo vệ cho chị.
Claire: Tôi thực sự biết ơn vì điều đó. Anh nghĩ anh cần bao lâu để phản hồi cho tôi với điều gì đó cụ thể?
Robert: Cho tôi đến thứ Năm. Tôi sẽ nói chuyện với bộ phận nhân sự và giám đốc trước đó. Tôi chỉ cần biết — chị có hạn chót cho đề nghị kia không?
Claire: Họ yêu cầu phản hồi trước thứ Hai tuần tới, vì vậy thứ Năm là hoàn toàn phù hợp. Tôi muốn đưa ra quyết định đúng đắn, không chỉ là quyết định nhanh.
Robert: Đó chính xác là cách tiếp cận đúng đắn. Tôi sẽ liên lạc với chị vào chiều thứ Năm, Claire. Cảm ơn chị đã thành thật với tôi.

questions.prompt: Why does Claire arrange this meeting with Robert?
options: She wants to ask for a salary review
She has received a job offer and wants to discuss it before deciding
She is planning to resign immediately
She wants to complain about her workload
questions.explanation: Claire says: 'I've received an offer from another company, and I haven't decided what to do yet.'
questions.hint: She explains her reason in the opening lines.

questions.prompt: How much higher is the salary in the new job offer?
questions.explanation: Claire says: 'The salary is about fifteen percent higher than what I'm on now.'

questions.prompt: According to Claire, what is the main reason she is considering the offer?
options: Wanting more growth and change in scope and responsibility
questions.explanation: Claire says: 'it's not the main driver. I feel like I've been doing similar work for about eighteen months without a significant change in scope or responsibility.'
questions.hint: She explicitly says salary is not the main driver.

questions.prompt: What does Robert reveal about the company's future plans?
options: That the company is about to be sold
That budgets are being cut across all departments
That a restructuring is planned for Q3 and Claire's name has come up
That a new performance review system will be introduced
questions.explanation: Robert says: 'There's a restructuring planned for Q3...and your name has genuinely come up in those conversations.'
questions.hint: Robert uses this to suggest there may be an opportunity for Claire internally.

questions.prompt: What specific internal opportunity does Robert hint at for Claire?
options: A new team lead role in the analytics function
A move to a different department
questions.explanation: Robert says: 'There will be a new team lead role created within the analytics function, and you'd be a strong candidate.'
questions.hint: He mentions a specific role title.

questions.prompt: Why does Claire say she would prefer to stay at the current company?
options: She does not want to go through a long notice period
She is worried the other company is financially unstable
She values the relationships she has built and knows the work well
questions.explanation: Claire says: 'I've built relationships with this team that I genuinely value, and I know the work well.'
questions.hint: She gives two reasons related to familiarity and relationships.

questions.prompt: What issue with working conditions does Claire raise apart from career progression?
options: The quality of the office environment
The lack of flexibility around working from home
The number of meetings she has to attend
The length of her annual leave allowance
questions.explanation: Claire says: 'One thing I'd mention is flexibility around working from home. Since the return-to-office policy changed, I've found the commute adds a lot of stress.'
questions.hint: She connects this to a recent policy change.

questions.prompt: What does the other company offer in terms of remote working?
options: Full remote working five days per week
Two extra remote days per week
questions.explanation: Claire says: 'The other company offers two extra remote days per week.'
questions.hint: She gives a specific number of days.

questions.prompt: What does Robert say he is willing to do about the remote working request?
options: Look into it and make a case for Claire with HR
Transfer Claire to a team that works more remotely
Offer her a choice between salary increase and remote work
questions.explanation: Robert says: 'I'd be willing to make a case for you' and that exceptions are being approved for certain roles.
questions.hint: He says the policy is set company-wide but there are exceptions.

questions.prompt: By what day does Robert say he will get back to Claire?
questions.explanation: Robert says: 'Give me until Thursday. I'll speak to HR and to the director by then.'

questions.prompt: When is Claire's deadline to respond to the other company's offer?
questions.explanation: Claire says: 'They've asked for a response by next Monday.'
questions.hint: She confirms the deadline after Robert asks.

questions.prompt: What principle does Claire express about making her final decision?
options: She wants to choose whichever option pays more
She will decide based on what her colleagues recommend
She wants to make the right choice, not just the fast one
She will wait to see if the internal promotion is confirmed before deciding
questions.explanation: Claire says: 'I want to make the right choice, not just the fast one.'
questions.hint: This is Claire's final statement before Robert closes the meeting.

cloze.template:
Claire: Thanks for making time for this, Robert. I know it was short notice. I wanted to talk to you because I've received an offer from another company, and I haven't decided what to do yet.
Robert: I appreciate you coming to me directly, Claire. That takes honesty. Can you tell me a bit about the offer — what kind of role is it?
Claire: It's a senior analyst position at a mid-sized consultancy. The salary is about fifteen percent higher than what I'm on now, and there's a clear {{b1}} to a management role within two years.
Robert: That's a meaningful offer. I won't pretend otherwise. Can I ask — what's prompting you to consider it? Is it mainly the salary, or are there other things that have been on your mind?
Claire: Honestly, it's a combination of things. The salary matters, but it's not the main {{b2}}. I feel like I've been doing similar work for about eighteen months without a significant change in scope or responsibility. I want to feel like I'm growing.
Robert: That's fair feedback, and I hear you. I want to be direct — some of what you're describing is something we can {{b3}} here. There's a restructuring planned for Q3 that I haven't been able to discuss externally yet, and your name has genuinely come up in those conversations.
Claire: I didn't know that. Can you say any more, or is it too early?
Robert: I can say that there will be a new team lead role created within the analytics function, and you'd be a strong candidate. I can't make a firm {{b4}} today, but I can push for an accelerated conversation internally this week.
Claire: I'd really appreciate that. The honest truth is I'd prefer to stay here if the {{b5}} looks right. I've built relationships with this team that I genuinely value, and I know the work well.
Robert: That means a lot, and I don't want to lose you. Let me also ask — is there anything beyond career progression that's been {{b6}} on you? Workload, flexibility, anything like that?
Claire: The workload is manageable. One thing I'd mention is flexibility around working from home. Since the return-to-office policy changed, I've found the commute adds a lot of stress to my week. The other company offers two extra {{b7}} days per week.
Robert: That's something I can look into. The policy is set at the company level, but there are exceptions being approved for certain roles and circumstances. I'd be willing to make a {{b8}} for you.
Claire: I'd be genuinely grateful for that. How long do you think you'd need to come back to me with something {{b9}}?
Robert: Give me until Thursday. I'll speak to HR and to the director by then. I just need to know — do you have a {{b10}} on the other offer?
Claire: They've asked for a response by next Monday, so Thursday works perfectly. I want to make the right choice, not just the fast one.
Robert: That's exactly the right way to approach it. I'll be in touch by Thursday afternoon, Claire. Thank you for being open with me.

blanks.explanation: 'A clear path to' is a standard career-related collocation meaning a clearly defined route toward a future role or goal.
'Main driver' is a business expression for the primary motivating factor behind a decision — Claire says salary is not the main driver.
'Address' in a professional context means to deal with or tackle a problem — Robert says the concerns Claire raised can be addressed internally.
'Firm commitment' is the exact phrase used — it means a definite, serious pledge that cannot easily be taken back.
'Trajectory' refers to the direction and speed of progress over time — Claire uses it to describe the overall career path within the company.
'Weighing on you' is an idiomatic expression meaning something is causing you worry or mental pressure — Robert uses it to invite Claire to share other concerns.
'Remote days' refers to days worked away from the office — Claire compares the remote working benefits of both options.
'Make a case for' means to argue or advocate in support of something — Robert says he is willing to advocate for Claire's remote working request.
'Something concrete' means something definite and actionable — not a vague response but a real, tangible answer.
'A deadline on the offer' means a final date by which a response must be given — Robert needs to know how much time he has to come back with a counter-proposal.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Claire is honest with her manager about receiving a competing offer. Some people believe this is a risky strategy — the company might feel pressured or even withdraw trust. Others argue it is the most professional approach. What do you think? Is it better to be transparent with an employer when considering leaving, or is it wiser to keep it private until a decision is made?
